subject: Physics
q: In materials like aluminium and copper,  the correct order of magnitude of various elastic modulii is :
question: In materials like aluminium and copper,  the correct order of magnitude of various elastic modulii is :
options: Young's modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> shear modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> bulk modulii
Bulk modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> shear modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> Young's modulii
Shear modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> Young's modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> bulk modulii
Bulk modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> Young's modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> shear modulii
solution: For aluminium and copper magnitude of Shear modulii is less than Young's modulii and bulk modulii is maximum.

subject: Physics
q: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
question: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
solution:
Elastic energy $=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{x}$
$$
\mathrm{F}=200 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{x}=1 \mathrm{~mm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \quad \therefore \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}=0.1 \mathrm{~J}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A cube has side length $5$ cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5$ N/m$^2$. The displacement produced by a force of $10$ N in the upper face of cube is _____ mm.
question: A cube has side length $5$ cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5$ N/m$^2$. The displacement produced by a force of $10$ N in the upper face of cube is _____ mm.
solution:
Given:
Side length of the cube, $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
Modulus of rigidity, $\eta = 10^5$ N/m$^2$
Force applied on the upper face, $F = 10$ N

Area of the upper face, $A = L^2 = (0.05)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m$^2$

Shear stress is given by:
$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{10}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4000$ N/m$^2$

Shear strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$

Modulus of rigidity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain:
$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{F/A}{\Delta x/L}$

Rearranging for displacement $\Delta x$:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot \eta}$

Substituting the values:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{10 \times 0.05}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^5}$

$\Delta x = \dfrac{0.5}{250} = 0.002$ m

Converting to millimeters:
$\Delta x = 0.002 \times 1000 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> have same volume and are made up of same material. If their area of cross sections are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, an extension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>l</mi></math> is produced. The force which is required to produce same extension in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is ______.
question: Two metallic wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> have same volume and are made up of same material. If their area of cross sections are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, an extension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>l</mi></math> is produced. The force which is required to produce same extension in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is ______.
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

From above equation, it follows that

Δl=FlAY   ...1

Again, volume of the wire is given by

V=Al⇒l=VA

Hence, from equation (1),

∆l=FVA2Y   ...2

As, Y and V is the same for both the wires,

∆l∝F A2

Hence, for two wires, it can be written that

∆l1∆l2=F1 A12×A22 F2   ...3

As ∆l1=∆l2, from equation (3), it follows that

F1 A22=F2 A12⇒F1 F2=A12 A22=412=16

subject: Physics
q: Under the same load, wire A having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches uniformly by the same amount as another wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and a cross section of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches. The ratio of the Young's modulus of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to that of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be:
question: Under the same load, wire A having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches uniformly by the same amount as another wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and a cross section of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches. The ratio of the Young's modulus of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to that of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be:
solution:
Elongation in uniform wire under load is given by, Δl=FlSY, where S is area of cross-section.

As F is same for both wire and Δl is also same, we can write

ΔlF=lSY⇒lASAYA=lBSBYB

⇒52.5×10-5×YA=63×10-5×YB

⇒YAYB=1

subject: Physics
q:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{V}=\frac{7 \times 10^6}{1.4 \times 10^{11}} \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^3 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{~V}=50 \mathrm{~mm}^3\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A copper wire of length $3\text{ m}$ is stretched by $3\text{ mm}$ by applying an external force. The volume of the wire is $600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$. The elastic potential energy stored in the wire in stretched condition would be _______ J.
(Given Young modulus of copper $= 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$)
question:
A copper wire of length $3\text{ m}$ is stretched by $3\text{ mm}$ by applying an external force. The volume of the wire is $600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$. The elastic potential energy stored in the wire in stretched condition would be _______ J.
(Given Young modulus of copper $= 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$)
solution:
Given parameters:
$L = 3\text{ m}$
$\Delta L = 3 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}$
$V = 600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$
$Y = 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$

Strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 10^{-3}$

Elastic potential energy stored in the wire is:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{Stress} \times \text{Strain} \times \text{Volume}$

Since $\text{Stress} = Y \times \text{Strain}$:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times (\text{Strain})^2 \times V$

Substituting the values:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (1.1 \times 10^{11}) \times (10^{-3})^2 \times (600 \times 10^{-6})$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-6} \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-1}$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 66 = 33\text{ J}$

Answer: $33$

subject: Physics
q: A 3 m long wire of radius 3 mm shows an extension of 0.1 mm when loaded vertically by a mass of 50 kg in an experiment to determine Young's modulus. The value of Young's modulus of the wire as per this experiment is $\mathrm{P} \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, where the value of P is: (Take $\left.\mathrm{g}=3 \pi \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question: A 3 m long wire of radius 3 mm shows an extension of 0.1 mm when loaded vertically by a mass of 50 kg in an experiment to determine Young's modulus. The value of Young's modulus of the wire as per this experiment is $\mathrm{P} \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, where the value of P is: (Take $\left.\mathrm{g}=3 \pi \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{50 \mathrm{~g}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^2}=\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} \\ & \frac{50 \times 3 \pi}{\pi \times\left(3 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2}=\mathrm{P} \times 10^{11} \times \frac{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\frac{50 \times 3 \times 3}{3^2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{11} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & \mathrm{P}=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two separate wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are stretched by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> respectively, when they are subjected to a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Assume that both the wires are made up of same material and the radius of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times that of the radius of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The length of the wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> can be expressed as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: Two separate wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are stretched by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> respectively, when they are subjected to a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Assume that both the wires are made up of same material and the radius of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times that of the radius of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The length of the wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> can be expressed as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
For AEπr2=y2 mma ...1
For B Eπ·16r2=y4 mm b ...2
∴ (1)/(2)
16=2 b4 a

ab=132
∴ =32

q: A solid sphere of radius r made of a soft material of bulk modulus K is surrounded by a liquid in a cylindrical container. A massless piston of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> floats on the surface of the liquid, covering entire cross-section of cylindrical container. When a mass m is placed on the surface of the piston to compress the liquid, the fractional decrement in the radius of the sphere <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , is:
question: A solid sphere of radius r made of a soft material of bulk modulus K is surrounded by a liquid in a cylindrical container. A massless piston of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> floats on the surface of the liquid, covering entire cross-section of cylindrical container. When a mass m is placed on the surface of the piston to compress the liquid, the fractional decrement in the radius of the sphere <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , is:
solution:
For sphere,∨=43πr3⇒∆∨∨=3∆rr⇒P=KΔVV
⇒P=3KΔRR⇒mg3Ka=ΔRR.

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical rod of length 1 m and radius 4 cm is mounted vertically. It is subjected to a shear force of $10^5 \mathrm{~N}$ at the top. Considering infinitesimally small displacement in the upper edge, the angular displacement $\theta$ of the rod axis from its original position would be : (shear moduli, $\left.\mathrm{G}=10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2\right)$
question: A cylindrical rod of length 1 m and radius 4 cm is mounted vertically. It is subjected to a shear force of $10^5 \mathrm{~N}$ at the top. Considering infinitesimally small displacement in the upper edge, the angular displacement $\theta$ of the rod axis from its original position would be : (shear moduli, $\left.\mathrm{G}=10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Shear moduli }=\frac{\sigma_{\text {shear }}}{\theta} \\ & 10^{10}=\frac{10^5}{\pi \times 16 \times 10^{-4}} \times \frac{1}{\theta} \\ & \theta=\frac{1}{160 \pi} \text { Radian }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of cross sectional area A, modulus of elasticity $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is stretched between two vertical rigid supports. When a mass of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended at the middle it sags lower from its original position making angle $\theta=\frac{1}{100}$ radian on the points of support. The value of $\mathrm{A}$ is _______ $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ (consider $x< < \mathrm{L}$ ). (given : $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
question: A wire of cross sectional area A, modulus of elasticity $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is stretched between two vertical rigid supports. When a mass of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended at the middle it sags lower from its original position making angle $\theta=\frac{1}{100}$ radian on the points of support. The value of $\mathrm{A}$ is _______ $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ (consider $x< < \mathrm{L}$ ). (given : $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
solution: In vertical direction $2 \mathrm{~T} \sin \theta=20$ using small angle approximation $\sin \theta=\theta$ $\begin{aligned} & \theta=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{10}{\theta} \\ & \mathrm{T}=1000 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{array}{ll} \text {Change in length } \Delta \mathrm{L} =2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{L}^2}-2 \mathrm{~L} \\ =2 \mathrm{~L}\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2 \mathrm{~L}^2}-1\right] \\ \Delta \mathrm{L} =\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~L}} \end{array}$ $\therefore$ Modulus of elasticity $=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}$ $\begin{aligned} & 2 \times 10^{11}=\frac{10^3}{\mathrm{~A} \times \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~L}}} \times 2 \mathrm{~L} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{A}=1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion (A) : Steel is used in the construction of buildings and bridges.
Reason (R) : Steel is more elastic and its elastic limit is high. 

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion (A) : Steel is used in the construction of buildings and bridges.
Reason (R) : Steel is more elastic and its elastic limit is high. 

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct
solution:
The relationship between Young's modulus, stress, and strain is:

stressstrain=Y

Since the elastic modulus of steel is more, for the same amount of stress, the strain will be less resulting in less deformation of buildings and bridges. Therefore, steel is used for construction of large structures. 

Hence, both assertion and reason are correct and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

subject: Physics
q: The normal density of a material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and its bulk modulus of elasticity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The magnitude of increase in density of material, when a pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied uniformly on all sides, will be :
question: The normal density of a material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and its bulk modulus of elasticity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The magnitude of increase in density of material, when a pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied uniformly on all sides, will be :
solution:
ρ=MV

dρρ=-dVV

k=-PdVV

-dVV=Pk

dρρ=Pk⇒dρ=ρPk

subject: Physics
q: A cube of metal is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>G</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>. The percentage change in the length of the side of the cube is close to : (Given bulk modulus of metal, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>)
question: A cube of metal is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>G</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>. The percentage change in the length of the side of the cube is close to : (Given bulk modulus of metal, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>)
solution:
B=-∆P∆VV
∆VV=-∆PB
Magnitude is given by,
∆VV=∆PB

Volume in terms of length can be represented as,

3×∆LL=∆PB

Therefore,

∆LL=13×∆PB

Substituting the values we get,
∆LL=13×4×1098×1010=160

Percantage change is,
160×100=53=1.67

subject: Physics
q: A metal wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has breaking stress <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached at one end of the string and is rotating in a horizontal circle. The maximum linear velocity of block will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A metal wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has breaking stress <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached at one end of the string and is rotating in a horizontal circle. The maximum linear velocity of block will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Here in horizontal circular motion, the only centripetal force is tension. So,T=mω2r=mv2l=10×v20.5=20v2

Now, Tmax= Breaking stress × Area

⇒20v2=5×108×10-4

⇒20v2=5×104

Thus, the maximum linear velocity of block is 

v=14104=50 m s-1

q: The elastic behaviour of material for linear stress and linear strain, is shown in the figure. The energy density for a linear strain of 5×10-4 is _____  kJ m-3. Assume that material is elastic upto the linear strain of 5×10-4,
question: The elastic behaviour of material for linear stress and linear strain, is shown in the figure. The energy density for a linear strain of 5×10-4 is _____  kJ m-3. Assume that material is elastic upto the linear strain of 5×10-4,
solution:
From the above graph, we can find that when the strain is 5×10-4 the stress is 100 MPa. We know that energy density is

U=12×stress×strain⇒U=12×100×106×5×10-4

⇒U=2.5×104 J m-3=25 kJ m-3

subject: Physics
q:
The diameter of a wire measured by a screw gauge of least count $0.001$ cm is $0.08$ cm. The length measured by a scale of least count $0.1$ cm is $150$ cm. When a weight of $100$ N is applied to the wire, the extension in length is $0.5$ cm, measured by a micrometer of least count $0.001$ cm. The error in the measured Young's modulus is $\alpha \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Ignore the contribution of the load to Young's modulus error calculation)
question:
The diameter of a wire measured by a screw gauge of least count $0.001$ cm is $0.08$ cm. The length measured by a scale of least count $0.1$ cm is $150$ cm. When a weight of $100$ N is applied to the wire, the extension in length is $0.5$ cm, measured by a micrometer of least count $0.001$ cm. The error in the measured Young's modulus is $\alpha \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Ignore the contribution of the load to Young's modulus error calculation)
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by:

$Y = \dfrac{F L_0}{A \Delta L} = \dfrac{4 F L_0}{\pi d^2 \Delta L}$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$Y = \dfrac{4 \times 100 \times 1.5}{\pi \times (0.08 \times 10^{-2})^2 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-2})}$

$Y = \dfrac{600}{\pi \times 64 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{15}{8\pi} \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$

The relative error in Young's modulus is:

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{\Delta L_0}{L_0} + 2\dfrac{\Delta d}{d} + \dfrac{\Delta (\Delta L)}{\Delta L}$

Substituting the least counts as absolute errors and the measured values:

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{0.1}{150} + 2 \times \dfrac{0.001}{0.08} + \dfrac{0.001}{0.5}$

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{1}{1500} + \dfrac{1}{40} + \dfrac{1}{500}$

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{2 + 75 + 6}{3000} = \dfrac{83}{3000}$

The absolute error in Young's modulus is:

$\Delta Y = Y \times \dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \left(\dfrac{15}{8\pi} \times 10^{11}\right) \times \dfrac{83}{3000}$

$\Delta Y = \dfrac{15 \times 83}{24000 \pi} \times 10^{11} = \dfrac{83}{1600 \pi} \times 10^{11} = \dfrac{83}{16\pi} \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$\Delta Y = \dfrac{83}{16 \times 3.14} \times 10^9 = \dfrac{83}{50.24} \times 10^9 \approx 1.65 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

Comparing this with $\alpha \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, we get $\alpha = 1.65$.

Answer: $1.65$

subject: Physics
q: A string $A$ of length $0.314$ m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass $0.8$ kg. The net change in length of the combination is _____ mm. (radius of both the strings is $0.2$ mm and acceleration due to gravity $= 10$ m/s$^2$) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
question: A string $A$ of length $0.314$ m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass $0.8$ kg. The net change in length of the combination is _____ mm. (radius of both the strings is $0.2$ mm and acceleration due to gravity $= 10$ m/s$^2$) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
solution:
Given:
Length of string A, $L_A = 0.314$ m
Young's modulus of string A, $Y_A = 2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Radius of both strings, $r = 0.2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
Length of string B, $L_B = 2L_A = 0.628$ m
Young's modulus of string B, $Y_B = 2Y_A = 4 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Mass of load, $m = 0.8$ kg
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$

Tension in both strings, $T = mg = 0.8 \times 10 = 8$ N

Cross-sectional area of both strings:
$A = \pi r^2 = 3.14 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^2$

Elongation in string A:
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{10}}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{8 \times 3.14 \times 10^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{10^2} = 10^{-3}$ m $= 1$ mm

Elongation in string B:
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{T L_B}{A Y_B}$
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{8 \times (2 \times 0.314)}{A \times (2 Y_A)} = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A} = 1$ mm

Net change in length of the combination:
$\Delta L = \Delta L_A + \Delta L_B = 1 + 1 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q:
The force required to stretch a wire of cross-section 1 cm2 to double its length will be :

(Given Yong's modulus of the wire =2×1011 N m-2)
question:
The force required to stretch a wire of cross-section 1 cm2 to double its length will be :

(Given Yong's modulus of the wire =2×1011 N m-2)
solution:
The wire is stretched to double its length. Therefore, if the length of the wore was l, change in the length ∆l=l.

Applying Hooke's law,

Y=stressstrain=FlA∆l⇒F=YA∆ll=YA⇒F=2×1011×1×10-4=2×107 N

=2×1011×10-42l-ll

=2×107 N

subject: Physics
q:
A uniform heavy rod of mass 20 kg. Cross sectional area 0.4 m2 and length 20 m is hanging from a fixed support. Neglecting the lateral contraction, the elongation in the rod due to its own weight is x×10-9 m. The value of x is _____ .
(Given. Young's modulus Y=2×1011Nm-2 and g=10 m s-2 )
question:
A uniform heavy rod of mass 20 kg. Cross sectional area 0.4 m2 and length 20 m is hanging from a fixed support. Neglecting the lateral contraction, the elongation in the rod due to its own weight is x×10-9 m. The value of x is _____ .
(Given. Young's modulus Y=2×1011Nm-2 and g=10 m s-2 )
solution:
Tension at a distance x from lower end =mglx

If the elongation in the element is taken as d∆l then, using Hooke's law,

Y=mgxdxAld∆l⇒d∆l=maxdxAlY⇒∫0∆ld∆l=∫0lmgxdxAlY⇒∆l=mgl2AY

⇒Δl=20×10×202×0.4×2×1011

⇒Δl=25×10-9

⇒x=25

q: The Young's modulus of a steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>11</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The wire is suspended from its support on a given planet. A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to the free end of the wire. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> of its value on the earth. The elongation of wire is (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> on the earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ):
question: The Young's modulus of a steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>11</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The wire is suspended from its support on a given planet. A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to the free end of the wire. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> of its value on the earth. The elongation of wire is (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> on the earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ):
solution:
The external force or tension F acting on the wire is its own weight. It is given by

 F= mg=4 kg×104 m s-2=10 N

The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is given by

Y= FA∆LL= FL∆LA..........................(1)

where, A is the cross-sectional area, L is the original length and ∆L is the elongation of the wire.

Substitute the given values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required elongation of the wire.

2×1011 N/m2= 10 N×6 m∆L×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2∆L= 10 N×6 m2×1011 N/m2×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2= 0.0001 m×103 mm1 m= 0.1 mm

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : The property of body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original shape when the external force is removed, is Elasticity.
Reason (R) : The restoring force depends upon the bonded inter atomic and inter molecular force of solid.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : The property of body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original shape when the external force is removed, is Elasticity.
Reason (R) : The restoring force depends upon the bonded inter atomic and inter molecular force of solid.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Elasticity is the ability of a material to deform under stress and then return to its original shape when the stress is removed.

The restoring force in elastic materials is associated with the inter-atomic or inter-molecular forces that act to bring the atoms or molecules back to their original positions when the material is deformed. When an external force is applied to a material, it causes a displacement of atoms or molecules from their equilibrium positions. The restoring force is a result of the potential energy stored in these inter-atomic or inter-molecular bonds, and it works to bring the material back to its original state.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)

subject: Physics
q: The increase in the pressure required to decrease the volume $(\Delta V)$ of water is $6.3 \times 10^7$ N/m$^2$. The percentage decrease in the volume is _____. (Bulk modulus of water $= 2.1 \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$.)
question: The increase in the pressure required to decrease the volume $(\Delta V)$ of water is $6.3 \times 10^7$ N/m$^2$. The percentage decrease in the volume is _____. (Bulk modulus of water $= 2.1 \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$.)
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by $B = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$

The fractional decrease in volume is $-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{B}$

Substituting the given values:

$-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{6.3 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-2}$

The percentage decrease in volume is $\left(-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \times 100$

Percentage decrease $= 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 3\%$

Answer: $3\%$

subject: Physics
q: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
question: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material is given by

Y=stressstrain=stress∆lL   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy U stored into the wire is given by

U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×stress×∆lL×A×L= 12×stress×A×∆l   ...2

From equation (1) and equation (2), it can be written that

U=12×Y×∆l2L×A   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the wire.

80 J=12×2.0×1011 Nm-2×0.02 m220 m×A⇒A= 2×80 J×202.0×1011×4×10-4 Nm-1=4×10-5 m2×106 mm21 m2= 40 mm2

subject: Physics
q: Steel ruptures when a shear of $3.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is applied. The force needed to punch a $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ diameter hole in a steel sheet $0.3 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick is nearly:
question: Steel ruptures when a shear of $3.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is applied. The force needed to punch a $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ diameter hole in a steel sheet $0.3 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick is nearly:
solution: Shearing strain is created along the side surface of the punched disk. Note that the forces exerted on the disk are exerted along the circumference of the disk, and the total force exerted on its center only. Let us assume that the shearing stress along the side surface of the disk is uniform, then $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{F}>& \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dF}_{\max }=\int_{\text {surface }} \sigma_{\max } \mathrm{dA}=\sigma_{\max } \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dA} \\ &=\int \sigma_{\max } \cdot \mathrm{A}=\sigma_{\max } \cdot 2 \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}}{2}\right) \mathrm{h} \\ &=3.5 \times 10^8 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2}\right) \times 0.3 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \pi \\ &=3.297 \times 10^4 \simeq 3.3 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: The Young's modulus of steel wire of radius $r$ and length $L$ is $Y$. If the radius $r$ and length $L$ of the wire are doubled then the value of $Y$
question: The Young's modulus of steel wire of radius $r$ and length $L$ is $Y$. If the radius $r$ and length $L$ of the wire are doubled then the value of $Y$
solution:
Young's modulus $Y$ is an intrinsic property of the material of the wire.

It depends only on the nature of the material and its temperature, and is independent of the dimensions of the wire such as length $L$ and radius $r$.

Therefore, when the radius and length of the steel wire are doubled, its Young's modulus remains unchanged.

Answer: remains unchanged

Now, breaking stress= Maximum lifting capacity  Area of cross section of rope 

⇒102.5×10-4=25 A

A=625×10-6

=6.25×10-4 m2

subject: Physics
q: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm, respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm, respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Young's modulus: $Y = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L/L}$.
When the same force is applied and both wires stretch by the same extension $\Delta L$, the ratio of Young's moduli is:
$\frac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \frac{F/A_A}{\Delta L/L_A} \times \frac{\Delta L/L_B}{F/A_B} = \frac{A_B}{A_A} \times \frac{L_A}{L_B}$
$=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} \times \frac{6.0}{5.4} = 1.5 \times \frac{10}{9} = \frac{5}{3}$.
Therefore $x : 3 = 5 : 3$, giving $x = 5$.
